The term voids refers to the vast, near-empty bubbles between galaxy clusters—regions where matter is scarce, and space stretches thinner than anywhere else.<[silence:600ms]>

Imagine a sponge in a bathtub.<[silence:600ms]> The water clings to the sponge’s strands (galaxies), while the hollows between them (voids) fill with nothing.<[silence:600ms]> Now drain the tub:<[silence:200ms]> the strands pull apart, and the hollows grow.<[silence:600ms]> That’s our universe.<[silence:600ms]> Dark energy acts like an invisible hand yanking the sponge wider, making voids swell faster than the space around galaxies.<[silence:600ms]> In the Local Void—a 150-million-light-year bubble next to our Milky Way—galaxies at its edge are being pushed away at 600 km per second, as if repelled by the emptiness itself.<[silence:600ms]> The emptier the void becomes, the stronger this "void repulsion" grows, a quirk of Einstein’s relativity.<[silence:600ms]>

Telescopes like the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument map these voids by tracking how galaxies scatter like leaves in a hurricane, tracing the wind of dark energy.<[silence:600ms]> The paradox?<[silence:200ms]> Voids make up 80% of the universe’s volume yet contain less than 10% of its atoms.<[silence:600ms]>

clean_story:
Cosmic emptiness expanding. Galaxies fleeing faster.

The term voids refers to the vast, near-empty bubbles between galaxy clusters—regions where matter is scarce, and space stretches thinner than anywhere else.

Imagine a sponge in a bathtub. The water clings to the sponge’s strands (galaxies), while the hollows between them (voids) fill with nothing. Now drain the tub: the strands pull apart, and the hollows grow. That’s our universe. Dark energy acts like an invisible hand yanking the sponge wider, making voids swell faster than the space around galaxies. In the Local Void—a 150-million-light-year bubble next to our Milky Way—galaxies at its edge are being pushed away at 600 km per second, as if repelled by the emptiness itself. The emptier the void becomes, the stronger this "void repulsion" grows, a quirk of Einstein’s relativity.

Telescopes like the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument map these voids by tracking how galaxies scatter like leaves in a hurricane, tracing the wind of dark energy. The paradox? Voids make up 80% of the universe’s volume yet contain less than 10% of its atoms.

Three related subjects are dark energy’s acceleration, cosmic web filaments, and how nothingness shapes galaxies.
